title: Duct System Startup
description: Put the duct system startup procedure in the correct order.

steps.text: Verify all duct connections are sealed and supported
Check that all dampers are in the open position
Install and inspect air filters
Start the air handler / fan unit
Measure airflow at each supply register (CFM)
Balance the system by adjusting branch dampers
Verify return air is adequate (no positive pressure)
Document final readings and close out

explanation: Startup sequence matters! Running the fan before checking connections can pressurize unsealed joints and blow insulation particles into the space. Always verify physical install before energizing.

title: Filter Replacement Procedure
description: Order the steps for replacing air filters in a commercial AHU.

steps.text: Turn off the air handler unit
Open the filter access panel
Note the filter size and type on the old filter
Remove old filters and bag them for disposal
Inspect filter rack for damage or gaps
Install new filters with airflow arrows pointing correct direction
Close access panel and ensure seal
Restart the unit and verify pressure drop across filters

explanation: Always turn off the unit before changing filters. Airflow arrows on filters must match the air direction. Verifying pressure drop ensures proper seating — too low means air is bypassing the filter.

title: Refrigerant Recovery
description: Sequence the steps for recovering refrigerant from an AC system before repair.

steps.text: Connect manifold gauges to system service ports
Read and record current system pressures
Connect recovery machine to manifold center hose
Connect recovery machine outlet to recovery cylinder
Open manifold valves and start recovery machine
Recover until system reaches required vacuum level
Close manifold valves and stop recovery machine
Weigh recovery cylinder and record amount recovered

explanation: EPA Section 608 requires proper refrigerant recovery. Venting refrigerant is illegal and carries heavy fines. Always record the amount recovered — it must match what goes back in or be reported.

title: Ductwork Leak Testing
description: Order the steps for performing a duct leakage test on a new install.

steps.text: Seal all supply registers and return grilles with tape/plastic
Connect duct pressurization fan to main trunk opening
Pressurize duct system to 1" W.C. (250 Pa)
Measure airflow through pressurization fan (CFM at test pressure)
Calculate leakage as percentage of design airflow
Walk the system and identify leak locations with smoke pencil
Seal identified leaks with mastic or approved sealant
Re-test to verify leakage meets specification

explanation: SMACNA and energy codes limit duct leakage to 4-6% of design airflow. Sealing ducts before testing tells you the baseline. Smoke pencils are the fastest way to find leaks once pressurized.

title: Condensate Drain Installation
description: Order the steps for installing a condensate drain on an AHU.

steps.text: Locate the drain pan connection on the AHU
Install a P-trap at the drain pan outlet
Run drain pipe with 1/4" per foot slope toward drain point
Install a secondary drain pan under the unit (if required)
Connect overflow switch to secondary pan
Pour water into primary pan to verify drainage
Verify overflow switch shuts down unit when triggered

explanation: The P-trap is critical — it prevents conditioned air from being blown out (positive pressure) or drawn in (negative pressure) through the drain line. Always test with actual water, never assume gravity works.

title: Chiller Startup Procedure
description: Order the steps for starting up a water-cooled chiller after off-season shutdown.

steps.text: Verify oil level and crankcase heater operation
Open condenser and evaporator water valves
Start condenser water pumps and verify flow
Start chilled water pumps and verify flow
Start cooling tower fans
Enable chiller controls and set temperature setpoint
Allow chiller to start and ramp up to load
Monitor operating pressures, temperatures, and amps for 30 minutes
Log all startup readings in the maintenance record

explanation: Water flow MUST be established before starting the chiller — running without flow freezes the evaporator (ice ball) or overheats the condenser. Oil heater ensures proper lubrication at startup.

title: Thermostat Replacement
description: Order the steps for replacing a thermostat with a new smart thermostat.

steps.text: Turn off power to HVAC system at breaker
Remove old thermostat cover and photograph existing wiring
Label each wire with its terminal letter (R, W, Y, G, C)
Disconnect wires from old thermostat
Remove old mounting plate from wall
Mount new thermostat backplate and level it
Connect labeled wires to matching terminals on new thermostat
Attach thermostat to backplate and power on system
Configure WiFi, test heating and cooling modes

explanation: Photographing and labeling wires BEFORE disconnecting is the most important step. Without it, you're guessing at wire assignments which can damage the new thermostat or HVAC equipment.

title: Air Balancing Procedure
description: Order the steps for balancing airflow in a multi-zone duct system.

steps.text: Review mechanical drawings for design CFM at each outlet
Verify the system is operating at design conditions (fan speed, filter, dampers)
Measure total system airflow at the AHU
Measure airflow at each supply diffuser with a balancing hood
Start with the outlet closest to the AHU and adjust its damper
Work outward, adjusting each branch damper to achieve design CFM
Re-measure all outlets after adjustments (changes affect the whole system)
Repeat adjustment cycle until all outlets are within ±10% of design
Lock damper positions and document final readings

explanation: Air balancing is iterative — adjusting one damper changes airflow everywhere else. Always start closest to the fan and work outward. Lock dampers when done to prevent tampering.

title: AC System Charge Check
description: Order the steps for checking refrigerant charge on a residential AC system.

steps.text: Verify indoor and outdoor units are running
Allow system to run for 15 minutes to stabilize
Connect manifold gauges to service ports
Read suction pressure and liquid pressure
Measure suction line temperature for superheat calculation
Measure liquid line temperature for subcooling calculation
Compare readings to manufacturer specifications
Add or recover refrigerant to match target superheat/subcooling

explanation: The system must stabilize before readings are meaningful. Superheat tells you evaporator performance, subcooling tells you condenser performance. Both must be checked — not just pressures alone.

title: Fire Damper Inspection
description: Order the steps for the required periodic fire damper inspection.

steps.text: Locate fire damper via access door in duct or above ceiling
Shut down the AHU serving the duct section
Visually inspect damper blades for damage, corrosion, or obstruction
Verify fusible link is intact and rated correctly
Manually trip the damper to verify full closure
Reset the damper and verify it locks in the open position
Document inspection date, damper location, and pass/fail status
Tag the damper with inspection date per NFPA 80 requirements

explanation: NFPA 80 requires fire damper inspection 1 year after installation and every 4 years after (every 6 years for hospitals). A failed fire damper in a fire event is catastrophic — this is life safety.

title: Union Apprenticeship: Duct Leakage Test
description: Order the steps for a SMACNA duct leakage test as taught in union apprenticeship training.

steps.text: Seal all openings — registers, grilles, and open ends — with caps or plastic and tape
Connect calibrated fan and duct tester apparatus to a main trunk opening
Pressurize the duct system to the specified test pressure (typically 1" W.C.)
Record baseline leakage reading from the duct tester (CFM at test pressure)
Walk the system and check for leaks using a smoke pencil or theatrical fog
Mark all identified leak locations and seal with mastic or approved sealant
Re-pressurize and retest to verify leakage is within SMACNA allowable limits
Document all results and submit the leakage test report to the engineer/GC

explanation: Duct leakage testing is a critical SMACNA apprenticeship skill. Union halls teach this procedure because energy codes now mandate duct leakage testing on most commercial projects. SMACNA standards limit leakage to CL 48, 24, 12, 6, or 3 depending on pressure class. Proper documentation is required for commissioning.

title: Union Certification: Fire Damper Inspection
description: Order the steps for an NFPA 80 fire damper inspection as taught in UA/SMACNA union training programs.

steps.text: Locate the fire damper on the building plans and identify its rating and type
Gain access to the damper via ceiling access panel or duct access door
Visually inspect the damper for physical damage, corrosion, or obstructions
Cycle the damper fully closed and fully open to verify free operation
Check the fusible link rating matches the fire-rated assembly requirement
Verify proper clearance between damper frame and blade — no binding or gaps
Document findings including damper location, condition, and pass/fail status
Attach an inspection tag to the damper with date, inspector name, and result

explanation: Fire damper inspection is a union certification skill taught in both UA and SMACNA programs. NFPA 80 requires inspection 1 year after installation and every 4 years after (6 years for hospitals). Union-trained inspectors are in high demand because many buildings are out of compliance. Proper tagging creates an audit trail that protects the building owner and the inspector.

title: TAB Procedure for Air Balancing
description: Order the steps for a Testing, Adjusting, and Balancing (TAB) procedure on a commercial air system per ASHRAE 111 and SMACNA TAB standards.

steps.text: Set up and calibrate all TAB instruments (manometer, anemometer, balancing hood)
Verify fan rotation direction and belt tension at each air handling unit
Measure total system airflow at the AHU discharge using a pitot tube traverse
Adjust main trunk dampers to distribute airflow proportionally to each branch
Balance branch duct dampers starting with the branch nearest the fan
Balance individual terminal devices (diffusers, registers) to design CFM
Verify minimum outdoor air intake meets ASHRAE 62.1 ventilation requirements
Document all final readings, damper positions, and fan operating data on the TAB report

explanation: The TAB procedure per ASHRAE 111 and SMACNA TAB standards follows a top-down approach: verify the fan delivers design airflow first, then balance from the trunk outward to terminals. Adjusting terminal devices before setting main dampers wastes time because every upstream change affects downstream airflow. Outdoor air verification is done after balancing because OA damper position interacts with system static pressure.

title: Duct Leakage Testing per SMACNA CL-6
description: Order the steps for performing a duct leakage test to SMACNA Seal Class A / Leakage Class 6 requirements.

steps.text: Seal all duct openings — registers, grilles, and open ends — with approved caps or plastic and tape
Connect calibrated duct leakage test fan and digital manometer to a main trunk opening
Pressurize the duct system to 1.5 times the design static pressure
Measure leakage rate in CFM through the test fan at the elevated test pressure
Compare measured leakage to the CL=6 allowable limit per SMACNA Duct Leakage Test Manual
If leakage exceeds CL-6, locate and repair leaks with mastic sealant or approved methods
Re-seal all openings and retest the repaired section to confirm compliance
Document test pressure, measured leakage, allowable leakage, and pass/fail on the leakage test report

explanation: SMACNA Leakage Class 6 (CL=6) is one of the tightest leakage standards, commonly specified on laboratory, hospital, and high-performance buildings. Testing at 1.5x design static pressure provides a safety margin. CL=6 limits leakage to 6 CFM per 100 square feet of duct surface area at the test pressure. Achieving CL-6 requires Seal Class A — all transverse joints, longitudinal seams, and duct wall penetrations must be sealed.

title: Fire-Rated Duct Installation
description: Order the steps for installing fire-rated ductwork through a fire-rated assembly per NFPA 90A and ISO 6944.

steps.text: Review drawings to verify where fire-rated duct construction is required
Install ductwork using minimum G-90 galvanized steel as required for fire-rated assemblies
Apply listed fire-resistive coating to a minimum thickness of 0.04 inches on all duct surfaces
Seal all transverse joints and longitudinal seams with fire-rated sealant
Install UL-listed fire dampers at each penetration through a fire-rated wall or floor
Test each fire damper for proper closure by tripping the fusible link mechanism
Label all fire-rated duct sections with the UL fire rating and listing number
Schedule and pass inspection by the authority having jurisdiction before concealing the assembly

explanation: Fire-rated duct installation per NFPA 90A requires a systems approach: the duct material (G-90 galvanized), fire-resistive coating (minimum 0.04" thick per ISO 6944), sealed joints, and fire dampers all work together to maintain the fire barrier. Every fire damper must be functionally tested before the ceiling or wall is closed. Labeling is required so future inspectors can verify the rating. This is life-safety work — shortcuts here kill people.

title: SACC IDF Room CRAC Unit Startup
description: Order the steps for starting up a Liebert MM40C CRAC unit in an IDF room on the SACC high-rise office project.

steps.text: Verify spring-neoprene isolation mounts are properly installed with 2" static deflection
Open chilled water isolation valves and verify flow through the CRAC coil
Confirm condensate drain P-trap is primed with water
Verify firestat is set to 125°F return air shutdown threshold
Power on the CRAC unit and initialize the iCOM controller
Set supply air temperature and humidity setpoints per the spec
Verify return air grille face velocity does not exceed 400 FPM
Confirm BTU meter is reading and transmitting data to BMS

explanation: CRAC unit startup follows a specific sequence: physical installation verification first (isolation, piping, drainage), then safety devices (firestat), then power-on and controls configuration, and finally performance verification (airflow, metering). Energizing the unit before confirming chilled water flow risks freezing the coil, and skipping the firestat check leaves a life-safety gap.

title: SACC Flex Duct Connection Procedure
description: Order the steps for making a flex duct connection at a branch takeoff on the SACC commercial office TI project.

steps.text: Verify flex duct run length will not exceed 5-foot maximum
Pull flex duct inner liner over the sheet metal collar
Secure inner liner with stainless steel worm-drive clamp
Apply UL-listed mastic over the inner clamp connection
Pull outer jacket and insulation over the connection
Secure outer jacket with a second SS worm-drive clamp
Verify flex duct is fully extended — no compression or kinks

explanation: The SACC specification is strict about flex duct connections: SS worm clamps (not zip ties or duct tape) and UL mastic are mandatory. The inner liner must be clamped and sealed before the outer jacket is pulled over. Measuring the run length first prevents wasted work — if it exceeds 5 feet, rigid duct must be used instead.

title: SACC Exhaust Fan Commissioning
description: Order the steps for commissioning a Greenheck CSP-A1050-VG exhaust fan system on the SACC high-rise project.

steps.text: Verify all exhaust branch ducts have backdraft dampers installed
Confirm fire dampers are installed and functional at all rated wall penetrations
Check that the exhaust fan is connected to timeclock control
Verify seismic spring-neoprene mounts are properly installed
Start the exhaust fan and verify rotation direction
Measure total exhaust airflow at 850 CFM using a pitot traverse
Balance individual branch ducts to design CFM at each grille
Document all readings and submit commissioning report to engineer

explanation: Exhaust fan commissioning on the SACC project follows a verification-then-performance approach. All accessories (backdraft dampers, fire dampers, timeclock, seismic mounts) must be confirmed before the fan is started. Starting the fan without backdraft dampers means air can reverse-flow through inactive branches. Timeclock control is verified before TAB because the fan must run during business hours regardless of zone occupancy.

title: SACC Turning Vane Installation in Rectangular Duct
description: Order the steps for installing double-thickness turning vanes in a rectangular duct elbow on the SACC high-rise TI project.

steps.text: Verify duct elbow dimensions match the approved shop drawing
Select turning vanes of minimum 20-gauge galvanized steel, double thickness
Install turning vane runners into the duct elbow
Verify no runner span exceeds 48 inches unsupported
Install intermediate support brackets where runner spans exceed 48 inches
Secure vane blades into runners at proper spacing for uniform airflow
Seal the duct elbow joints with mastic and verify no air leakage

explanation: Turning vane installation on the SACC project requires double-thickness vanes at 20-gauge minimum with a 48-inch maximum unsupported runner span. Checking the span before installing vane blades is critical — once the blades are in place, adding intermediate supports is much more difficult. These specs are stricter than standard SMACNA because the SACC project is in a seismic zone where vane deflection under dynamic loads is a concern.

title: SACC MDF Room CRAC Seismic Installation
description: Order the steps for installing a Liebert MM92C CRAC unit with full seismic restraint in the MDF room of the SACC high-rise (Los Angeles, Seismic Design Category D).

steps.text: Review structural engineer's approved seismic anchorage details and verify floor capacity
Install OSHPD-approved seismic base frame with spring-neoprene isolation mounts
Set CRAC unit on isolation mounts and verify minimum 2-inch static deflection
Install lateral seismic restraint snubbers at all four corners of the unit
Connect chilled water piping with flexible connectors at unit connections
Install flexible electrical conduit and connections to allow seismic movement
Verify all connections have adequate flexibility to accommodate 1-inch seismic displacement
Submit seismic installation certification to structural engineer and AHJ for approval

explanation: Seismic CRAC installation in a Los Angeles high-rise requires coordination between the mechanical and structural trades. The isolation mounts provide vibration control during normal operation, while the seismic snubbers limit unit movement during an earthquake. All piping and electrical connections must use flexible connectors — rigid connections will rupture during seismic movement, causing flooding and power loss in the MDF. OSHPD approval and engineer certification are required before the installation is accepted.
